codeReview.summary: Comprehensive cross-area integration test file (1289 lines, 16 test functions) covering all 4 required areas. The implementation correctly tests: (1) Unmarshal->Marshal validity with 4 config variations including cluster, simple.conf, mixed types, and array-of-maps; (2) v1-v2-v1 compatibility with 10 sample configs from v1 parse_test.go via a custom marshalMapToConfig helper; (3) Full modify pipeline with file I/O, port change verification, and field-by-field unchanged-field assertions; (4) AST vs struct round-trip fidelity with 6 tests documenting comment/variable/include/formatting preservation differences. All 16 tests pass. Code quality is high with thorough doc comments documenting the distinction between AST and struct round-trip paths. The helper functions (marshalMapToConfig, writeMap, writeValue, writeArray, compareMapValues) are well-structured and handle all NATS value types including time.Time (ISO8601), bcrypt passwords, and size suffixes.

issues.description: marshalMapToConfig iterates over map[string]any using Go's non-deterministic map iteration order. While this doesn't cause test failures (compareMapValues does recursive key-by-key comparison rather than string comparison), it means test output is non-deterministic. Sorting keys would make debugging easier if a test ever fails.

TestCrossASTPreservesFormattingStructNormalizes has a complex separator check: `if !strings.Contains(astText, "port = ") || !strings.Contains(astText, "port =")` followed by a fallback line-by-line scan. The OR condition is always true when 'port = ' is absent (since 'port =' is a substring of 'port = '). The logic is correct but unnecessarily convoluted; a single line-by-line scan checking for '=' in the port line would be clearer.

TestCrossASTPreservesIncludesStructExpands uses different inputs for the AST path (with include directive) and struct path (resolvedInput without include). This is justified since include files don't exist in the test directory, but it means the test doesn't verify actual include expansion behavior end-to-end. The test comments document this limitation well and reference emit_test.go for full include round-trip validation.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The test file is named cross_test.go while AGENTS.md testing guidance says 'Use descriptive names: lex_test.go, parse_test.go, unmarshal_test.go, marshal_test.go, emit_test.go'. The name cross_test.go follows this convention well. However, the task description referenced 'cross_area_test.go' which doesn't match the actual filename. This is a minor reference inconsistency in the task, not a code issue.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Task says 'Specifically look at cross_area_test.go' but actual file is conf/v2/cross_test.go per git show ac3803f42 --stat

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker followed the go-library-worker skill procedure correctly: read existing v2 code first, wrote comprehensive tests, ran all 4 verification steps (go test v2, go vet, go test v1, go build). The handoff reports skillFeedback.followedProcedure=true with no deviations. The worker did not strictly follow TDD (write failing tests first) since these are integration tests that compose already-implemented functions, which is a reasonable deviation for this feature type.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows worker read all v2 files (marshal.go, unmarshal.go, emit.go, compat.go, ast.go) before writing the test file. All 4 verification commands passed. Handoff confirms no deviations.

summary: PASS. The cross-area integration test file comprehensively covers all 4 required test areas and fulfills VAL-CROSS-001, VAL-CROSS-002, VAL-CROSS-003, and VAL-CROSS-006. All 16 tests pass. The implementation includes well-documented helper functions for map serialization and recursive value comparison. The code quality is high with thorough doc comments explaining the AST vs struct round-trip distinction. Three non-blocking issues noted: non-deterministic map iteration in the helper, a complex separator check logic, and the include test using different inputs for AST and struct paths. None affect correctness.
